1: YHWH commands Moses to chisel new tablets and make a wooden chest — the covenant is renewed and housed. The ark as the container of the renewed covenant is a type of Christ as the dwelling of God with his people. Paul uses the ark's mercy seat (kapporeth) directly in Romans 3:25: "God presented Christ as a hilastērion [mercy seat/propitiation] through faith in his blood." The place where covenant and atonement meet — the ark's lid where blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement — is the type Paul applies to the cross. Direct type (Romans 3:25).
2: "I will write on the tablets the words that were on the first tablets, which you broke." The broken covenant is renewed without alteration — the same words, the same demands. God's covenant standard does not lower after Israel's failure; it is re-established. The new covenant similarly does not lower the moral standard but provides what the old covenant could not: the Spirit who writes the law on the heart (Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 3:3). The renewal of the tablets is the shadow; the Spirit-written heart is the fulfillment. Shadow.
3: Moses makes the ark of acacia wood — the incorruptible desert timber used throughout the tabernacle. The ark of incorruptible wood houses the covenant word. As a shadow: Christ's body does not see corruption (Acts 2:27, 31 — Peter cites Psalms 16:10 of the resurrection: "you will not let your Holy One see corruption"). The incorruptible material of the ark points forward to the incorruptible one who permanently houses God's presence. Shadow.
4: YHWH writes the same Ten Commandments on the new tablets — the covenant faithfully re-established. God's word stands despite human breaking; his covenant purposes do not collapse under the weight of Israel's failure. The resurrection is the NT parallel: what seemed destroyed on Good Friday is fully restored and confirmed on Easter morning. "All the promises of God find their Yes in him" (2 Corinthians 1:20). Revelation of God.
5: The tablets are placed in the ark, where they remain. The covenant document housed in the covenant container — the word of God at the heart of Israel's life and worship. Christ as the Word made flesh (John 1:14) is the fulfillment: the covenant word is no longer in a chest carried through the wilderness but incarnate in a person who tabernacled among us. The ark that housed the stone tablets is the shadow; the person of Christ is the substance. Shadow.
6: Aaron dies at Moserah and Eleazar his son takes over the priestly ministry. The death of the high priest requiring succession. Hebrews 7:23–25 makes the contrast with Christ explicit: "The former priests were many in number, because they were prevented by death from continuing in office, but he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever. Consequently, he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them." Aaron's death and succession is the type whose weakness reveals the need for an indestructible priest. Direct type (Heb 7).
7: The journey from Gudgodah to Jotbathah, a land of flowing streams. A transitional travel note. Even in movement through wilderness stages, YHWH accompanies his people to better ground. The promise of the streams — the flowing water in the desert — echoes Isaiah 35:6–7 and Deuteronomy 41:18 (streams in the desert as eschatological renewal). In Christ: "Whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again" (John 4:14). The journey toward flowing streams is the type; Christ as the source of living water is the fulfillment. Theme.
8: The LORD sets apart Levi to carry the ark, stand before the LORD, minister to him, and pronounce blessings in his name — the three dimensions of Levitical calling. These three are fulfilled and transcended in Christ: he carries (and is) God's covenant presence, he is the eternal priest who ministers before the Father in the heavenly sanctuary (Hebrews 8:1–2), and through him all blessing flows to the nations (Galatians 3:14; Ephesians 1:3). The Levitical calling is the shadow; Christ's priesthood is the substance. Type.
9: "The LORD himself is his inheritance." Levi receives no land allotment — YHWH himself is Levi's portion. This is the highest inheritance formula in the OT, echoing Numbers 18:20 (Aaron: "I am your portion and your inheritance among the Israelites") and Psalms 16:5 ("LORD, you are my portion and my cup"). In Christ, this inheritance is extended to every believer: "the riches of his glorious inheritance in the saints" (Ephesians 1:18); "God himself will be with them" (Revelation 21:3). God-as-inheritance moves from the priestly tribe to the entire people of God. Shadow.
10: Moses' second forty days on the mountain; YHWH listens again and is unwilling to destroy. The persistence of divine mercy — the same grace extended twice after the same rebellion. "Where sin increased, grace abounded all the more" (Romans 5:20). YHWH's repeated mercy toward the stubborn is not weakness but the character that reaches its definitive expression in the cross: "God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us" (Romans 5:8). Revelation of God.
11: "Get up and lead the people on their journey so that they may go in and take possession of the land." The resumption of the covenant mission after discipline and restoration. The pattern of failure, intercession, renewal, and resumed mission is the structure of both the OT covenant and the believer's life in Christ. The risen Christ resumes his disciples after their failures: "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations" (Matthew 28:19). The mission continues through the Mediator's restoration. Theme.
12: "What does the LORD your God ask of you except to fear the LORD your God, to walk in all his ways, to love him, and to serve the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul." The summary of the covenant demand — the practical content of the Shema. Jesus quotes the love-command from Deuteronomy 6:5 and Leviticus 19:18 as the two greatest commandments (Matthew 22:37–40), building directly on this verse. Christ himself embodies this summary perfectly — the one who fears God (Hebrews 5:7), walks in God's ways without stumbling (1 Peter 2:22), loves God utterly (John 14:31), and serves God to the point of death (Philippians 2:8). The covenant demand Christ fulfills and, through the Spirit, enables in his people (Romans 8:4). Direct connection.
13: "Keep the LORD's commandments and statutes that I am commanding you today for your own good." The commandments are for Israel's benefit — the Creator's design for creaturely flourishing, not arbitrary imposition. Jesus' "I came that they may have life and have it abundantly" (John 10:10) continues this connection between God's word and fullness of life. The law's purpose is always the good of those who receive it; Christ is the fulfillment of that purpose. Theme.
14: "To the LORD your God belong the heavens, even the highest heavens, the earth and everything in it." The cosmic ownership declaration — the entire creation belongs to YHWH. Paul cites this principle (from Psalms 24:1) in 1 Corinthians 10:26: "the earth is the Lord's, and everything in it." In Christ, this cosmic lordship is exercised through the one to whom "all authority in heaven and on earth has been given" (Matthew 28:18) and through whom all things were created (Colossians 1:16). The Creator's ownership is expressed through the Son's universal lordship. Revelation of God.
15: "Yet the LORD delighted in your fathers and loved them, and he chose you, their descendants, above all peoples." Election by delight and love — the basis is YHWH's sovereign affection, not Israel's merit or size. Ephesians 1:4–5: "In love he predestined us for adoption to himself as sons through Jesus Christ, according to the purpose of his will." The election of Israel in the patriarchs is the OT type; the election of the church in Christ is the fulfillment. Both rest on the same foundation: divine delight and choice, not human achievement. Revelation of God.
16: "So circumcise your hearts and stop being stubborn." The call to inner transformation — the law commands what only God can accomplish. Deuteronomy anticipates its own unfulfillment here: the stubborn people are told to un-stubborn themselves, which they cannot do. Jeremiah 4:4 repeats the call; Ezekiel 36:26 promises what it demands ("I will remove the heart of stone"); Romans 2:29 calls it "circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the letter." In Christ, the Spirit effects the inner transformation the law could only command. Direct type fulfilled in Christ through the Spirit.
17: "For the LORD your God is God of gods and Lord of lords — the great, mighty, and awesome God." The title "Lord of lords" (ʾădōnê hāʾădōnîm) belongs to YHWH alone in the OT. In Revelation 17:14 and Deuteronomy 19:16, this title is applied to Jesus Christ: "he is Lord of lords and King of kings." The title that Deuteronomy declares belongs to the incomparable God of Israel is given to the crucified and risen Jesus, identifying him with YHWH in the strongest terms available. Direct NT connection (Revelation 17:14; Deuteronomy 19:16).
18: "He upholds justice for the fatherless and the widow, and loves the foreigner, providing them with food and clothing." YHWH's character as the defender and provider for the most vulnerable. This is the ethical character of God that Jesus enacts in his ministry — feeding the hungry, welcoming the outsider, defending the powerless — and that he presents as the basis of final judgment in Matthew 25:31–46 ("I was hungry and you gave me food... I was a stranger and you welcomed me"). The God who cares for the fatherless and foreigner meets humanity in the face of those who need his care. Revelation of God enacted in Christ.
19: "So love the foreigner, for you were foreigners in the land of Egypt." Compassion grounded in memory of one's own alienation. Paul applies this exact logic to the Gentiles' inclusion in Christ: "remember that at that time you were without Christ, excluded from citizenship in Israel... But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far away have been brought near by the blood of Christ" (Ephesians 2:11–13). The foreigners who love foreigners because they were once foreign themselves is the OT prototype; the formerly excluded Gentiles who welcome the excluded is the NT fulfillment. Direct conceptual connection (Eph 2).
20: "Fear the LORD your God. Serve him, hold fast to him, and take oaths in his name." "Hold fast" (dāḇaq) is the language of covenantal adhesion — the same word used for the survivors of Baal Peor (Deuteronomy 4:4). In Christ, this holding fast is enabled by union rather than command alone: "Abide in me, and I in you" (John 15:4). What Deuteronomy commands as an act of the will, Christ establishes as a relationship of mutual indwelling. The command to cling is fulfilled in the vine-and-branches union. Theme.
21: "He is your praise and he is your God — the one who has done these great and awe-inspiring things for you." YHWH as the content of Israel's praise — not merely the recipient of praise but himself the substance praised. Hebrews 2:12 applies Psalms 22:22 to Christ: "I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise." The crucified and risen Christ sings the praise of God in the assembly of his people — the one who is God's praise-object becomes, through the Son, the one who leads the praise. Theme.
22: "Your fathers went down to Egypt as seventy people, and now the LORD your God has made you as numerous as the stars of the sky." The Abrahamic promise of star-like multiplication (Genesis 15:5) fulfilled in the Exodus generation. The promise kept is the ground of confidence for greater promises. In Christ, the children of Abraham are extended beyond ethnic Israel to all who share Abraham's faith: "he is the father of us all" (Romans 4:16), and the final gathering encompasses "every nation, tribe, people and language" (Revelation 7:9) — a multitude that fulfills what the stars of the sky only shadowed. Theme.
